doc_intro_p1: In a digital environment saturated with AI-generated or AI-assisted content, transparency is no longer optional—it is a seal of trust. The TCP/UP protocol (Transparent Content Protocol / User-Powered) allows you to clearly declare the role of humans and AI in the production of content, in a simple, readable, and responsible manner.
doc_intro_p2: This guide walks you step by step through applying the protocol, from analyzing your creative process to integrating visual and technical signatures, including choosing the right label.
doc_intro_warning: Important: TCP/UP is a declarative protocol for transparency on AI usage. It is neither technical proof, third-party certification, nor legal advice. It aims to make the editorial process more readable for the public.
doc_intro_note: Note: This charter is a civic initiative based on good-faith declaration and author responsibility.
doc_s1_heading: 1. Analyze Your Creative Process - The Decision Tree (The 30-Second Rule)
doc_s1_intro: Before applying a label, determine where your work sits in the human/AI spectrum using this logical flow. Need help? Find your label in 30 seconds with the choice assistant
doc_s1_q1: 1. Did AI generate, rephrase, or correct part of the content?
doc_s1_q1_li1: NO → [TCP/UP: HUC] (no AI used)
doc_s1_q1_li2: YES → proceed to question 2
doc_s1_q2: 2. Did AI modify the meaning or structure of ideas (rephrasing, restructuring, introducing new ideas)?
doc_s1_q2_li1: NO (intervention limited to spelling/grammar) → [TCP/UP: HCA]
doc_s1_q2_li2: YES → proceed to question 3
doc_s1_q3: 3. Did the substance of the content (intention, structure, argument) remain entirely designed by humans?
doc_s1_q3_li1: Yes, the substance is human and AI only helped formulate or structure the expression → [TCP/UP: HCE]
doc_s1_q3_li2: No, AI contributed to the plan, ideas, angle, or content structure → proceed to question 4
doc_s1_q4: 4. Was the content co-created with AI or primarily generated by it?
doc_s1_q4_li1: Co-creation with substantial human intervention → [TCP/UP: ACE]
doc_s1_q4_li2: Generation mostly delegated to AI, with limited human validation or selection → [TCP/UP: AIC]
doc_s1_variant: Variant: If you provided a very detailed prompt already describing the intellectual architecture, arguments, and tone, HCE may be considered. Out of caution, if AI nonetheless influenced the final organization, introduced new angles, or shaped the text structure, ACE remains preferable.
doc_s1_prudence: Principle of Caution: If you hesitate between two labels, choose the one giving greater credit to AI. Transparency trumps pride.
doc_s2_huc_def: No AI used in the creation process
doc_s2_huc_use: text, image, or document created without AI assistance
doc_s2_hca_def: AI limited to spelling, grammar, or syntax correction
doc_s2_hce_def: Human substance, form or formatting assisted by AI
doc_s2_ace_def: AI contributed to the substance of the content alongside humans
doc_s2_aic_def: Content mostly or entirely generated by AI
doc_s2_aic_use: raw generation, automatic summary, unreviewed translation
doc_s2_example_huc: HUC: a literary manuscript typed without any assistance, an editorial where every word is carefully chosen, a digital drawing created by hand without AI filters.
doc_s2_example_hca: HCA: a thesis reviewed by an AI proofreader without style modification, a legal contract reviewed for typos.
doc_s2_example_hce: HCE: meeting notes transformed into a structured report, an internal email rephrased to be more diplomatic.
doc_s2_example_ace: ACE: an SEO article 50% generated by AI, then rewritten and enriched with human expertise, a business plan with AI-provided structure, filled in by humans.
doc_s2_example_aic: AIC: a logo generated by prompt, a Bash script created entirely by AI, raw translation of a technical manual.
doc_s2_cas_particuliers: Special Cases: For more ambiguous situations (translation, code, brainstorming, mega-prompt…), see section Critical Cases and Arbitration (section 4 of the full documentation).
doc_s3_intro: TCP/UP offers three levels of signatures, to be used according to publication context.
doc_s3_1_text: Download official badges from the Resources section. Insert them into your content visibly, for example at the top or bottom of the article, next to the title, or in the footer.
doc_s3_1_best_practices: Best Practices: Link the badge to the TCP/UP charter to explain the label. Respect the original colors and proportions. Avoid modifying badges (resizing is permitted).
doc_s3_2_text: For environments where an image cannot be inserted (RSS feeds, technical documentation, metadata), use the text form: [TCP/UP: XXX] (replace XXX with HUC, HCA, HCE, ACE, AIC).
doc_s3_2_badge_demo: [TCP/UP: HCE] - Human substance, form assisted by AI.
doc_s3_3_intro: To push transparency further, you can integrate the signature directly into your page's source code. This approach is optional today, but it prepares the ground for future tools (search engines, AI assistants, archives) that could recognize and use these metadata.
doc_s3_3_meta_generator: Find the metadata generator in the Resources section.
doc_s3_3_1_text: Insert one or more <meta> tags in the <head> of your HTML page. The structure is designed to be readable, extensible, and independent of content type.
doc_s3_3_1_info_li1: Variable Content Key: it identifies the media type (e.g., editorial, visual, code). This is the only part that changes depending on the labeled component.
doc_s3_3_1_info_li2:
Fixed Suffixes:
  • content-origin - origin of Substance (intention, ideas, structure)
  • form-origin - origin of Form (formulation, execution)
  • ai-role - specific role played by AI
doc_s3_3_1_info_li3: Standardized Values: the tables below detail the permitted values for each label.
doc_s3_3_ck_editorial_usage: Article, blog post, essay, report, text documentation
doc_s3_3_ck_code_usage: HTML, CSS, JavaScript, script, program source code
doc_s3_3_ck_transcription_usage: Automatic transcription of audio or video
doc_s3_3_ck_synthesis_usage: Summary, automatic synthesis of a document
doc_s3_3_2_text: Today, no browser or search engine specifically reads these custom tags. But as transparency becomes central, standards will likely emerge. Richer formats like JSON‑LD (used by Schema.org) or data-* attributes could then be adopted by major platforms. For now, a simple <meta> tag suffices to:
doc_s3_3_2_li1: Make your commitment more visible by leaving a declarative trace in the source code
doc_s3_3_2_li3: Anticipate ecosystem evolution without reworking all your content
doc_s3_3_2_advice: Advice: if you publish content in technical environments (databases, RSS feeds), you can also add the text signature [TCP/UP: XXX] in a dedicated field (for example a category or tag). This makes the information exploitable by your own tools.
doc_s4_1_intro1: The hybridization of workflows between human intelligence (HI) and artificial intelligence (AI) sometimes creates situations where the boundary between Substance (Fond) and Form (Forme) becomes subtle. The credibility of the TCP/UP protocol rests on the ability to resolve these ambiguities with common sense and consistency. This section offers guidance for authors facing the most frequent cases, without attempting to exhaust all situations.
doc_s4_1_rappel: Reminder: TCP/UP is a civic initiative, a common language based on good faith. It is neither official certification nor a technical proof tool. The goal is to provide an honest reading framework for the reader, by holding the author accountable.
doc_s4_2_title: 4.2 Analysis of Scenarios and Edge Cases
doc_s4_2_1_li1: Description: Original human text is translated to another language by AI, then reviewed by the author to verify fidelity
doc_s4_2_1_li2: Content/Form Analysis: Substance (C) remains entirely human. Form (F) is generated by AI and validated by humans
doc_s4_2_1_li4: Justification: The idea is human, but the syntactic structure and lexical choice of the target language are produced by the algorithm
doc_s4_2_1_li5: Recommendation: Any language change through automated tools generally excludes HUC and HCA labels (reserved for unassisted form)
doc_s4_2_2_li1: Description: Author asks AI to rephrase a paragraph. AI introduces a new metaphor or argument not originally present, which the author decides to keep
doc_s4_2_2_li2: Content/Form Analysis: AI has influenced Substance (C) by injecting new concepts or perspectives
doc_s4_2_2_li4: Justification: Original intent was modified by AI's suggestive contribution
doc_s4_2_2_li5: Recommendation: When AI brings conceptual elements unforeseen by the author and these are retained, content more faithfully falls into the A series, as AI contributed to substance
doc_s4_2_3_title: 4.2.3 AI-Generated Content Then Heavily Restructured by Humans
doc_s4_2_3_li1: Description: User generates complete text via AI, then modifies much of it to inject their style, sources, and correct errors
doc_s4_2_3_li2: Content/Form Analysis: Substance (C) is co‑constructed (AI structure, human enrichment). Form (F) is mostly human
doc_s4_2_3_li4: Justification: Initial spark and first architecture come from the machine. Human acts as curatorial editor
doc_s4_2_3_li5: Principle: The "blank page" logic remains determinant here: if AI provided the first intellectual structure, returning to the H series is generally not the most transparent choice
doc_s4_2_4_title: 4.2.4 AI Brainstorming and Independent Human Writing
doc_s4_2_4_li1: Description: Author uses AI to generate an outline or ideas, then closes the tool and writes the text entirely without assistance
doc_s4_2_4_li2: Content/Form Analysis: Substance (C) is hybrid (shared ideas). Form (F) is human-sourced
doc_s4_2_4_li4: Justification: Intellectual structure was assisted, even if execution is human
doc_s4_2_4_li5: Recommendation: Assistance with Substance (outline, theses, structure) leads to ACE for full transparency on idea genesis
doc_s4_2_5_title: 4.2.5 Computer Code: Generation, Debugging, and Optimization
doc_s4_2_5_li1: Description: Developer generates a function via AI, then corrects, optimizes, and integrates it
doc_s4_2_5_li2: Content/Form Analysis: Algorithmic logic (C) is shared. Syntax (F) is generated by AI
doc_s4_2_5_li4: Justification: In programming, Substance and Form are often inseparable. Correction does not obscure initial generation
doc_s4_2_5_li5: Recommendation: For code, when a logical block is generated by AI then adapted by humans, ACE is generally the most faithful label
doc_s4_2_6_li1: Description: Human writes an exhaustive prompt (often over 500 words) that precisely structures the thought architecture: guiding ideas, detailed arguments, specific sources, desired tone. AI executes complete wording
doc_s4_2_6_li2: Content/Form Analysis: Substance (C): Human (intellectual content) | Form (F): AI (syntax, writing)
doc_s4_2_6_li3: Recommended Label: HCE (Human‑Centric Editing) may be considered. Out of caution, if AI nonetheless influenced final organization, introduced new angles, or shaped text structure, ACE remains preferable
doc_s4_2_6_vigilance: Vigilance Point, Risk of "Prompt-washing": Claiming HCE for content generated via mega-prompt is legitimate IF AND ONLY IF the prompt already contains the entire intellectual architecture (thesis, arguments, structure, tone). A 500-word prompt saying "write me an article about X" is NOT a mega-prompt. It's a generation instruction → AIC.
doc_s4_2_6_sincerity: Sincerity Test: Could you publish your prompt alone as a detailed outline? If yes → HCE possible. If no → ACE or AIC.
doc_s4_2_7_title: 4.2.7 Publication Without Complete Review (The "Blind Output" Case)
doc_s4_2_7_li1: Description: User generates complete content via AI and publishes it without thorough critical review or fact verification
doc_s4_2_7_li2: Content/Form Analysis: Substance (C) and Form (F) are delegated to automation
doc_s4_2_7_li4: Justification: Without point-by-point human validation, the author cannot guarantee content integrity. The process is then purely generative
doc_s4_2_7_li5: Recommendation: Without active review and substantial validation, AIC is the most coherent label
doc_s4_2_8_li1: Description: Project uses multiple specialized AI (text, image, code, etc.) whose results are integrated
doc_s4_2_8_li3: Justification: If project is perceived as a unit, global label should reflect the most automated component
doc_s4_2_9_li1: Description: Content initially labeled AIC is reworked over multiple versions until substantially modified
doc_s4_2_9_li2: Arbitration Rule: Label reflects current state, not history. However, mention of evolution is encouraged
doc_s4_2_9_li3: Suggested Formulation: "Current Version: [TCP/UP: HCE] (evolution from AIC, see history)"
doc_s4_2_9_li4: Recommendation: Transparency changelog MAY be maintained for evolving documents
doc_s4_2_10_li1: Description: Document produced by multiple contributors with heterogeneous AI usage
doc_s4_2_10_li2: Arbitration Rule: Global label SHOULD reflect the most automated component (principle of caution)
doc_s4_2_10_li3: Alternative: Per-section labeling with contributor mention
doc_s4_2_10_li4: Recommendation: For collective publication, designate a "transparency officer" to validate final label
doc_s4_3_li1: 1. Principle of Caution (Conservatism): Hesitating between two labels, author should choose the one giving greater credit to AI
doc_s4_3_li2: 2. Blank Page Rule: If AI provided the first logical structure (outline, architecture), content should avoid H-series labels
doc_s4_3_li3: 3. Sincerity Audit: "Diagonal" human validation is generally insufficient to justify ACE or HCE label. Active review is encouraged
doc_s4_3_li4: 4. Code Indivisibility: For code, when a logical block is generated by AI then adapted by humans, ACE is generally the most faithful label
doc_s4_4_title: 4.4 Technical Granularity of ACE Label (Internal Use)
doc_s4_4_intro: These sub-categories are optional and intended for internal or documentary use. They do not modify the public displayed label.
doc_s4_4_ace_l_desc: AI assists with substance but human directs roughly three-quarters of the structure.
doc_s4_4_ace_b_desc: Actual balance. Fluid co-writing between human and tool.
doc_s4_4_ace_d_desc: AI generates majority of substance and form, human acts as curator.
doc_s4_4_impl: Implementation Recommendation: These distinctions can be integrated into data attributes (e.g., data-tcpup-sub="ACE-B"), but the displayed visual badge must remain the standard label ACE.
doc_s4_5_li1: Self-Declaration: TCP/UP is a declarative system based on honor. It does not constitute cryptographic technical proof
doc_s4_5_li2: Declaration and Good Faith: Standard relies on author's responsible self-declaration. It privileges assumed transparency over illusion of perfect detection
doc_s4_5_li3: Responsibility: Applying a TCP/UP label commits author's credibility. Manifest bad faith harms collective trust
doc_s4_5_closing: This section is an arbitration guide, not an absolute constraint. It will evolve with community feedback and practices.
